refined_fr.refined_fr: The NGHE solution shall enable measurement and reporting of energy consumption per unit of work output (energy_per_ton) to support evaluation against a customer-defined energy efficiency target.
refined_fr.rationale: The original FR conflates two distinct obligations: (1) the capability to measure/report energy efficiency metrics, and (2) the achievement of a performance target. The refined FR focuses on the functional requirement—providing visibility and traceability of the energy efficiency metric—which is solution-neutral and within system control. Achievement of the target (E_target ≤ energy_per_ton) is a verification criterion, not a functional requirement, since it depends on operational context, equipment design, and worksite conditions beyond the NGHE solution's direct control. This separation clarifies that the system must instrument and expose the metric; stakeholders (Site Manager, Safety Manager, Maintenance/Quality) then use that data to assess performance.
refined_fr.verification_idea: Verify that the NGHE solution collects energy consumption data (kWh or equivalent) and work output data (tons moved) over the agreed evaluation period, computes energy_per_ton, and reports it in a format accessible to authorized stakeholders. Confirm that reported values are traceable to source sensors and that the calculation method is documented and reproducible.
sub_frs: FR-NGHE-003.1: The NGHE solution shall measure and log total energy consumed (in standardized units) during operation over the evaluation period.
FR-NGHE-003.2: The NGHE solution shall measure and log total work output (in tons moved) during operation over the evaluation period.
FR-NGHE-003.3: The NGHE solution shall compute the energy efficiency metric (energy_per_ton) from logged energy and work output data.
FR-NGHE-003.4: The NGHE solution shall provide access to energy efficiency metric data and supporting logs to authorized stakeholders (Site Manager, Maintenance/Quality) for performance evaluation.

candidate_dps: DP1: Instrument energy input (fuel/electricity) at source; instrument work output (tonnage moved) at point of delivery; compute ratio offline post-evaluation period; report against target.
DP2: Embed real-time energy and tonnage sensors; stream data to onboard compute module; calculate energy_per_ton continuously; trigger alerts if threshold breached; log for post-evaluation reporting.

DP3: Integrate energy metering at power distribution node; integrate tonnage measurement via load cells or displacement sensors; synchronize timestamps; aggregate over evaluation window; compute and report energy_per_ton with confidence bounds.

DP4: Establish energy consumption baseline via controlled calibration runs; measure incremental energy and tonnage during operational cycles; normalize for ambient/operational variance; report delta energy_per_ton against target.

DP5: Decompose energy consumption into sub-system contributions (propulsion, hydraulics, auxiliary); measure tonnage per sub-system; compute energy_per_ton per subsystem and aggregate; enable root-cause analysis of efficiency gaps.
selected_dp.selected_dp: DP3: Integrate energy metering at power distribution node; integrate tonnage measurement via load cells or displacement sensors; synchronize timestamps; aggregate over evaluation window; compute and report energy_per_ton with confidence bounds.

potential_sources: Energy metering and tonnage measurement are logically independent (different physical phenomena, different sensor modalities); decoupling via timestamp synchronization avoids tight temporal coupling.
Aggregation and reporting logic is decoupled from sensor acquisition; allows independent evolution of measurement hardware and data processing.
Confidence bounds computation introduces weak coupling to data quality assumptions (sensor calibration, synchronization precision); manageable via explicit interface contract.
DP3 avoids coupling to specific propulsion technology or work cycle pattern; applicable across equipment variants.
coupling_check.notes: DP3 exhibits lower coupling than DP2 (which embeds real-time compute, tightening hardware-software coupling) and DP5 (which couples to sub-system decomposition, increasing measurement complexity). DP1 and DP4 are viable but DP1 lacks real-time visibility and DP4 introduces calibration overhead. DP3 balances independence, observability, and simplicity.

assumptions_risks: ASSUMPTION: Energy source is centralized and metered at a single point (e.g., fuel tank, power inlet). RISK: Multi-source energy (e.g., hybrid propulsion, auxiliary generators) may require distributed metering; mitigation: design sensor architecture to support multi-point aggregation.

ASSUMPTION: Tonnage measurement is feasible via load cells or displacement inference. RISK: Some work outputs (e.g., compaction, grading) may not have direct tonnage proxy; mitigation: define work output metric in collaboration with customer; may require DP revision.

ASSUMPTION: Timestamp synchronization between energy and tonnage streams is achievable to required precision (e.g., ±1 second). RISK: Sensor clock drift or network latency may degrade accuracy; mitigation: implement NTP or GPS-based synchronization; validate sync precision during commissioning.

ASSUMPTION: Evaluation period is sufficiently long (days to weeks) to average transient effects and operational variance. RISK: Short evaluation windows or highly variable duty cycles may yield unreliable energy_per_ton; mitigation: establish minimum evaluation duration and variance thresholds in acceptance criteria.

ASSUMPTION: Customer-defined E_target is achievable and based on validated baseline data. RISK: Unrealistic targets or lack of baseline may lead to false failures; mitigation: conduct pre-evaluation baseline study; document target derivation.

ASSUMPTION: Worksite safety plan permits installation of metering hardware (e.g., load cells on chassis, energy taps at power distribution). RISK: Safety constraints may restrict sensor placement; mitigation: engage safety review early; design non-intrusive measurement alternatives (e.g., wireless load sensing).

ASSUMPTION: Data logging and reporting infrastructure is available (onboard storage, connectivity for upload). RISK: Offline or remote worksites may lack connectivity; mitigation: design local buffering and batch upload capability.
selected_dp.recommended_revision: FR should be refined to explicitly state: (1) Definition of 'work output' (tonnage moved, distance, cycles, or proxy metric); (2) Evaluation period duration and operational variance tolerance; (3) Measurement precision and confidence level required (e.g., ±5% energy_per_ton); (4) Data logging and reporting interface (local storage, cloud upload, real-time dashboard); (5) Constraints from worksite safety plan on sensor placement and power draw. DP3 should be elaborated to specify: (a) Energy metering location and modality (fuel flow, electrical power, or hybrid); (b) Tonnage measurement method (load cells, displacement, or inference); (c) Timestamp synchronization protocol and precision; (d) Aggregation window (e.g., hourly, daily, evaluation-period); (e) Confidence bounds calculation method (e.g., sensor uncertainty propagation). This will enable clear DP -> IM mapping and reduce ambiguity in implementation.

im_boundary.name: NGHE Solution
im_boundary.scope: Centralized energy metering, tonnage measurement integration, timestamp synchronization, aggregation logic, and efficiency metric computation with confidence bounds reporting.

external_interfaces.name: Telemetry
external_interfaces.details: Transmit computed energy_per_ton metrics, confidence bounds, and aggregation summaries to external monitoring/analytics systems.

external_interfaces.name: Tasking
external_interfaces.details: Receive evaluation window parameters, metering configuration, and sensor calibration directives from task orchestration layer.

external_interfaces.name: Network Link
external_interfaces.details: Communicate with Vehicle and Teleoperation subsystems to retrieve real-time power distribution node data, load cell readings, and displacement sensor inputs; synchronize clock references.

module_containment_scores.notes: Excellent fit. NGHE Solution is the natural home for energy efficiency analytics and metering integration. Aggregation, synchronization, and metric computation are core to the solution domain. Minor diffusion: sensor hardware instantiation resides in Vehicle; power distribution node hardware in physical plant. Data flows inbound via Network Link; results outbound via Telemetry.

Poor fit. Vehicle owns sensor hardware (load cells, displacement sensors) but not the metering logic, aggregation, or efficiency computation. Placing DP here would scatter analytics across vehicle firmware and require heavy coupling to external reporting. Computation logic would diffuse to NGHE Solution anyway.

Very poor fit. Teleoperation is a control/interface layer, not an analytics or metering domain. Energy metering and efficiency computation have no cohesion with remote operation logic. Severe diffusion: all core logic would leak to NGHE Solution; only UI presentation might reside here.

Very poor fit. Safety Services handles hazard detection and alerts, not energy efficiency metrics. While energy efficiency could indirectly relate to safe operation, the primary DP (metering, aggregation, efficiency computation) has no safety-critical dependency. Placing it here creates false cohesion and high diffusion to NGHE Solution.

assigned_im.containment_hypothesis: The DP is fundamentally an energy efficiency analytics function. NGHE Solution is the designated domain for such analytics, metering integration, and performance reporting. The DP logically decomposes into: (1) sensor data acquisition (Vehicle provides raw inputs via Network Link), (2) timestamp synchronization and aggregation (NGHE Solution logic), (3) metric computation (NGHE Solution logic), (4) confidence bound calculation (NGHE Solution logic), and (5) reporting (Telemetry interface). This decomposition keeps the core analytics and decision logic tightly contained within NGHE Solution while maintaining clean data flow boundaries.
containment_risks: Sensor calibration and hardware configuration (load cells, displacement sensors) reside in Vehicle; NGHE Solution must receive well-formed, calibrated data streams. Risk: if sensor drift or miscalibration occurs, NGHE Solution's confidence bounds may be invalid. Mitigation: establish clear sensor health/calibration status signals in Network Link interface.

Timestamp synchronization depends on clock alignment between Vehicle, power distribution node, and NGHE Solution. Risk: clock skew or network latency could corrupt aggregation windows. Mitigation: define explicit time-sync protocol in Network Link; include timestamp quality metadata in telemetry.

Evaluation window parameters (duration, aggregation frequency) are task-driven inputs. Risk: if Tasking interface does not clearly specify window semantics, NGHE Solution may compute metrics over ambiguous intervals. Mitigation: formalize evaluation window schema in Tasking interface contract.

Confidence bound computation requires statistical model of sensor uncertainty and aggregation error. Risk: if model assumptions are not validated against real sensor behavior, bounds may be overly optimistic or pessimistic. Mitigation: include sensor uncertainty metadata in Network Link; validate bounds empirically during commissioning.

fr_dp_gate.reason: DP3 directly addresses the FR by specifying a complete solution path: energy metering at power distribution node + tonnage measurement via sensors + timestamp synchronization + aggregation + metric computation with confidence bounds. The DP is sufficiently detailed to enable design and verification against the acceptance criterion (energy_per_ton ≤ E_target). No logical gaps detected between requirement and design parameter.

diffusion_risks: Sensor hardware instantiation (load cells, displacement sensors) resides in Vehicle module, but DP logic (aggregation, synchronization, computation) resides in NGHE Solution. Risk: if sensor calibration status or health signals are not explicitly communicated via Network Link interface, NGHE Solution cannot validate data quality before computing confidence bounds. Mitigation: formalize sensor health/calibration metadata in Network Link contract.

Timestamp synchronization logic is distributed across Vehicle (sensor acquisition), power distribution node (energy metering), and NGHE Solution (aggregation). Risk: clock skew or network latency could corrupt evaluation windows. Mitigation: define explicit time-sync protocol and include timestamp quality metadata in Network Link interface.

Confidence bound computation depends on statistical models of sensor uncertainty. Risk: if uncertainty metadata is not reliably transmitted from Vehicle/power distribution node to NGHE Solution, bounds may be invalid. Mitigation: establish formal sensor uncertainty schema in Network Link; validate empirically during commissioning.

Evaluation window parameters (duration, aggregation frequency) flow inbound via Tasking interface. Risk: ambiguous window semantics could lead to incorrect metric computation. Mitigation: formalize evaluation window schema in Tasking interface contract with explicit start/end time definitions and aggregation frequency semantics.
dp_im_gate.containment_notes: NGHE Solution achieves 0.92 containment score with 0.08 diffusion. This is excellent. The core DP logic (aggregation, synchronization, metric computation, confidence bounds) is tightly contained within NGHE Solution. Sensor hardware and power distribution node hardware are appropriately owned by Vehicle and physical plant respectively; NGHE Solution consumes their outputs via clean Network Link interface. Telemetry interface cleanly exports results. No alternative module (Vehicle, Teleoperation, Safety Services) offers better cohesion or lower diffusion. Ownership allocation is sound.

fr_im_gate.verification_feasibility: High. The acceptance criterion (energy_per_ton ≤ E_target over agreed evaluation period) is directly measurable from NGHE Solution's computed metric. Verification requires: (1) validation that energy metering at power distribution node is accurate and calibrated, (2) validation that tonnage measurement via load cells/displacement sensors is accurate and calibrated, (3) validation that timestamp synchronization maintains required precision, (4) validation that aggregation logic correctly sums energy and tonnage over evaluation window, (5) validation that confidence bounds are statistically sound. All verifications are feasible within NGHE Solution's scope and can be performed during commissioning and operational testing.
fr_im_gate.allocation_clarity: Clear. FR is allocated to NGHE Solution IM with well-defined external interfaces: Tasking (inbound: evaluation window parameters, metering configuration, sensor calibration directives), Network Link (inbound: power distribution node data, load cell readings, displacement sensor inputs, clock references), and Telemetry (outbound: energy_per_ton metrics, confidence bounds, aggregation summaries). Data flow boundaries are explicit. Sensor hardware ownership (Vehicle) and power distribution node ownership (physical plant) are clearly separated from analytics logic ownership (NGHE Solution). No ambiguity in allocation.

recommended_revisions: Formalize sensor health/calibration status signals in Network Link interface contract. Define schema for sensor drift indicators, calibration timestamps, and uncertainty estimates. NGHE Solution must reject or flag metrics computed from uncalibrated or drifted sensors.

Define explicit time-sync protocol in Network Link interface. Specify clock reference source (e.g., GPS, NTP), acceptable clock skew tolerance, and timestamp quality metadata (e.g., confidence interval on timestamp accuracy). Include mechanism for NGHE Solution to detect and report clock synchronization failures.

Formalize evaluation window schema in Tasking interface contract. Specify: (1) window start/end time semantics (wall-clock vs. operational time), (2) aggregation frequency and boundary alignment, (3) handling of partial windows or incomplete data, (4) expected latency for window closure and metric reporting.

Document statistical model for confidence bound computation. Specify: (1) assumed sensor uncertainty distributions, (2) aggregation error model, (3) confidence level (e.g., 95%), (4) validation approach during commissioning. Include sensitivity analysis for model assumptions.

Establish commissioning protocol to validate: (1) energy metering accuracy against known loads, (2) tonnage measurement accuracy against calibrated weights, (3) timestamp synchronization precision under operational conditions, (4) confidence bound empirical coverage (i.e., verify that true energy_per_ton falls within reported bounds at claimed confidence level).

Add explicit requirement to Tasking interface: NGHE Solution shall reject or flag evaluation windows with insufficient data quality (e.g., sensor health issues, clock skew exceeding tolerance, incomplete aggregation intervals). Define escalation path for such flags.

decision.justification: All three gates (FR-DP, DP-IM, FR-IM) pass with high confidence, and the DP is sufficiently detailed to enable design and verification. However, the gate results expose critical diffusion risks and interface contract gaps that must be resolved before proceeding to sub-FR decomposition. The recommended revisions are not minor clarifications—they are essential architectural decisions that affect data quality, timing precision, and confidence bound validity. Given the high safety criticality of this FR (energy efficiency metric used for operational decision-making in a mining context), these gaps must be closed at the current level. Proceeding to sub-FR decomposition without formalizing sensor health signaling, time-sync protocols, and evaluation window semantics would propagate ambiguity into lower-level designs and create verification blind spots. The DP logic itself is sound, but its realization depends on explicit interface contracts that are currently underspecified.

revise.why: The DP must be augmented with explicit specifications for: (1) sensor health/calibration status signal flow via Network Link (schema, validation rules, rejection criteria), (2) time-sync protocol and timestamp quality metadata (clock reference, skew tolerance, quality indicators), (3) evaluation window semantics and aggregation boundary alignment (wall-clock vs. operational time, partial window handling, data completeness thresholds), (4) confidence bound computation model with documented assumptions and empirical validation approach. These are not sub-FRs; they are refinements to the current DP that must be captured in interface contracts (Network Link, Tasking) and design specifications before sub-FR decomposition. The DP currently states 'aggregation + metric computation with confidence bounds' but does not specify how data quality, timing precision, and window semantics are enforced—these are essential to the correctness of the solution.

assumptions: Energy metering at power distribution node is accurate and calibrated; calibration status is communicated to NGHE Solution.
Tonnage measurement via load cells and displacement sensors is accurate and calibrated; sensor health and uncertainty metadata are available.
Timestamp synchronization across Vehicle, power distribution node, and NGHE Solution maintains precision sufficient for evaluation window aggregation (tolerance to be defined in revised DP).
Evaluation window parameters (start/end time, aggregation frequency) are unambiguously defined and communicated via Tasking interface.
Confidence bound computation assumes known sensor uncertainty distributions and aggregation error models; these are validated empirically during commissioning.
NGHE Solution has authority to reject or flag metrics computed from data with insufficient quality (sensor drift, clock skew, incomplete aggregation).
Network Link interface is reliable and provides metadata (sensor health, timestamp quality, calibration status) alongside sensor readings.
open_risks: Sensor calibration drift during operational life: if calibration status is not continuously monitored and communicated, NGHE Solution may compute metrics from drifted sensors without awareness. Mitigation: formalize sensor health/calibration metadata in Network Link contract; define drift detection thresholds and escalation rules.

Clock skew or network latency corrupting evaluation windows: if timestamp synchronization is not explicitly specified and validated, aggregation windows may be misaligned or overlapping. Mitigation: define time-sync protocol with explicit clock reference, skew tolerance, and timestamp quality metadata; include detection and reporting mechanism in NGHE Solution.

Ambiguous evaluation window semantics: if window start/end times are not clearly defined (wall-clock vs. operational time), or if aggregation frequency boundaries are not aligned, metric computation may be incorrect or non-reproducible. Mitigation: formalize evaluation window schema in Tasking interface with explicit semantics and handling rules for partial windows.

Confidence bound invalidity: if sensor uncertainty models are not empirically validated or if assumptions are violated in operation, reported confidence bounds may not reflect true coverage. Mitigation: document statistical model assumptions; establish commissioning protocol to validate empirical coverage; include sensitivity analysis.

Data quality escalation ambiguity: if NGHE Solution lacks clear authority or escalation path to reject/flag low-quality metrics, operational decisions may be made on unreliable data. Mitigation: add explicit requirement to Tasking interface defining rejection criteria and escalation path.

Interface contract drift: if Network Link, Tasking, and Telemetry interface contracts are not formally documented and version-controlled, implementation may diverge from design intent. Mitigation: establish formal interface specification documents with schema definitions, validation rules, and change control.
evidence_needed: Formal specification of Network Link interface contract: schema for sensor readings, sensor health/calibration metadata, timestamp quality indicators, uncertainty estimates. Include validation rules and rejection criteria.

Formal specification of time-sync protocol: clock reference source (GPS, NTP, or other), acceptable clock skew tolerance (in milliseconds or microseconds, depending on evaluation window granularity), timestamp quality metadata format, mechanism for NGHE Solution to detect and report sync failures.

Formal specification of Tasking interface contract: evaluation window schema (start/end time semantics, wall-clock vs. operational time, aggregation frequency, boundary alignment, handling of partial windows, expected latency for window closure).

Statistical model documentation for confidence bound computation: assumed sensor uncertainty distributions, aggregation error model, confidence level (e.g., 95%), sensitivity analysis for model assumptions.

Commissioning protocol and test plan: validation of energy metering accuracy, tonnage measurement accuracy, timestamp synchronization precision, confidence bound empirical coverage. Include acceptance criteria for each validation.
Operational monitoring specification: rules for detecting sensor drift, clock skew, incomplete aggregation, and escalation procedures. Define thresholds and alerting mechanisms.

Empirical validation results from pilot or prototype deployment: evidence that confidence bounds achieve claimed coverage, that timestamp synchronization maintains required precision, that sensor health signals enable reliable data quality assessment.
